coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of phosphorous-doped graphene quantum dots/bismuth iron vanadate composite as bifunctional electrocatalysts for water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Babu, Beena Mol
Kim, Min Jae
Sharma, Tata Sanjay Kanna
Choi, Won Mook
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we have prepared a mixed metal oxide of bismuth iron vanadate (BiFeV) and phosphorous-doped graphene quantum dots (P-GQDs) by hydrothermal technique and fabricated the composite using BiFeV and P-GQDs (PG-BiFeV). Incorporating P-GQDs provides a higher number of electrochemically active areas and stable active sites, preventing the degradation of the catalyst over time and enhancing overall performance. The prepared PG-BiFeV composite demonstrates excellent bifunctional electrocatalytic activities for hydrogen evolution (HER) and oxygen evolution reactions (OER). Specifically, the PG-BiFeV composite delivers superior overpotentials of 341 mV for HER and 396 mV for OER at the current density of 100 mA cm−2 with good long-term stability, which is 11 % and 12 % enhancement for HER and OER by the incorporation of P-GQDs compared to BiFeV. Based on the bifunctional HER and OER activities, the PG-BiFeV composites are employed the symmetric electrode system for the overall water electrolysis. The PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrode system shows superior water-splitting performance with lower cell voltage and good long-term stability. These results can demonstrate the potential applications of PG-BiFeV composites as the cathode and anode electrode materials in the development of water electrolysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bismuth iron vanadate
Graphene quantum dots
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Oxygen evolution reaction
Water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of BiFeV and PG-BiFeV composite

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b) Raman spectra of BiFeV, P-GQDs, and PG-BiFeV composite.
XPS spectra of BiFeV, P-GQDs, and PG-BiFeV composite: (a) Survey spectrums and the comparison of spectra for (b) Bi4f, (c) Fe2p, (d) V2p/O1s, (e) P2p, and (f) C1s spectrums.
FE-SEM images of (a) BiFeV and (b) PG-BiFeV composite. (c) EDS elemental mapping images of PG-BiFeV composite. (d–f) TEM images of PG-BiFeV composite with SAED pattern.

(a) HER LSV curves of the prepared BiV, BiFeV, and PG-BiFeV composite at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and (b) the corresponding Tafel plots, and (c) the Nyquist plots for HER. (d) The HER CV curves of PG-BiFeV composite for different scan rates in non-faradaic region and (e) the corresponding plots for current density vs. square root of scan rates.
(a) The HER chronoamperometry curve of PG-BiFeV composite at 0.2 V for 100 h operation and (b) their LSV curves before and after the chronoamperometric measurement 2000 cycles.

(a) OER LSV curves of the prepared BiV, BiFeV, and PG-BiFeV composite, (b) the corresponding Tafel plots, and (c) the Nyquist plots for OER. (d) The OER CV curves of PG BiFeV composite for different scan rates in non-faradaic region and (e) the corresponding plots for current density vs. square root of scan rates.
(a) The OER chronoamperometry curve of PG-BiFeV composite at 0. 15 V for 100 h operation and (b) their LSV curves before and after the chronoamperometric measurement 2000 cycles.

(a) Schematic illustration of an overall splitting electrolyzer using PG-BiFeV composites as the cathode and anode and (b) the photo image for the generation of H2 and O2 gas bubbles on the PG-BiFeV electrodes during water splitting. (c) LSV curves of overall water splitting using PG-BiFeV composites and (d) their chronoamperometry curve for overall water splitting 100 h operation. The inset shows the HER LSV curves using PG-BiFeV composites before and after the chronoamperometry measurement.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of PG-BiFeV composite.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of phosphorous-doped graphene quantum dots/bismuth iron vanadate composite as bifunctional electrocatalysts for water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44160, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, University of Ulsan, Daehak-ro 93, Nam-gu, Ulsan, 44160, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we have prepared a mixed metal oxide of bismuth iron vanadate (BiFeV) and phosphorous-doped graphene quantum dots (P-GQDs) by hydrothermal technique and fabricated the composite using BiFeV and P-GQDs (PG-BiFeV). Incorporating P-GQDs provides a higher number of electrochemically active areas and stable active sites, preventing the degradation of the catalyst over time and enhancing overall performance. The prepared PG-BiFeV composite demonstrates excellent bifunctional electrocatalytic activities for hydrogen evolution (HER) and oxygen evolution reactions (OER). Specifically, the PG-BiFeV composite delivers superior overpotentials of 341 mV for HER and 396 mV for OER at the current density of 100 mA cm−2 with good long-term stability, which is 11 % and 12 % enhancement for HER and OER by the incorporation of P-GQDs compared to BiFeV. Based on the bifunctional HER and OER activities, the PG-BiFeV composites are employed the symmetric electrode system for the overall water electrolysis. The PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrode system shows superior water-splitting performance with lower cell voltage and good long-term stability. These results can demonstrate the potential applications of PG-BiFeV composites as the cathode and anode electrode materials in the development of water electrolysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The escalating use of conventional energy sources has caused severe environmental pollution, climate change, and energy shortages, necessitating the pursuit of eco-friendly and clean energy alternatives. Hydrogen (H2), as an environmentally benign energy source, has emerged as a promising substitute for fossil fuels, devoid of carbon emissions and environmental harm. While hydrogen has been applied in practical applications, particularly in residential and transportation sectors, efficient and environmentally friendly hydrogen production remains a pivotal technical challenge limiting its widespread adoption. Currently, numerous studies focus on synthesizing effective H2 production with low carbon emissions. In this regard, water electrolysis emerges as a promising and preferred technology due to its inherent attractive traits, such as ease of operation, environmental friendliness, and efficiency in generating hydrogen. In the water electrolysis process, noble metals and their oxides, including Pt, Ir, and Ru, have demonstrated remarkable catalytic activity for H2 generation. However, their high cost and limited availability impede their widespread practical utilization. Thus, the development of earth-abundant, cost-effective, and highly active catalysts as alternatives to noble metal-based catalysts remains a significant challenge [1–6]. Transition metal oxides, particularly vanadium-based materials, have gained considerable attention due to their unique properties and potential applications in diverse fields, including catalysis, energy storage, and optics [7–10]. Transition metal vanadates (TMVs), such as bismuth vanadate (BiVO4), iron vanadate (FeVO4), copper vanadate (Cu3V2O8), and manganese vanadate (Mn2V2O7), have been widely used as catalysts, sensors, solar cells, and in batteries [11–15]. Vanadate has been considered for its potential use in electrochemical applications due to its redox properties and its ability to interact with metal ions and induce electrochemical reactions. The structural properties of vanadates, including crystal structure and morphology, can significantly impact their electrochemical properties [16–19]. Because of their multivalent oxidation states, which help to achieve multi-electron transfer and strong V-O bond strength, mixed metal oxides are a potent candidate for electrochemical applications. Specifically, BiVO4 has gained significant attention in electrochemical applications due to its favorable electronic, optical, and chemical properties. Its non-toxicity, compatibility, and favorable surface engineering methods such as doping and heterojunction formation with other catalysts make it a versatile and most studied material [20–22]. However, the utilization of BiVO4 is hindered by inherent challenges, including poor electrical conductivity, lower specific surface area, and limited active sites. Moreover, it often requires higher overpotential to drive electrochemical reactions limiting their practical use. To overcome these limitations and optimize its performance for targeted applications, efforts are underway to develop mixed metal vanadates with enhanced properties [16–22]. Several mixed metal vanadates, such as Bi x Y1-x VO4 [23], Bi1-x Pr x VO4 [24], Bi0.5Dy0.5VO4 [25], and Bi1-x Sm x VO4 [26], have been documented in recent literature. Incorporating transition metals into bismuth vanadate, such as Fe, has been explored to modify its electronic structure. The 3d orbitals of Fe introduce additional energy levels that influence the bandgap, valence band maximum, and conduction band minimum. This modification promotes charge separation and enhances the multivalent oxidation states of both vanadium and Fe [27]. Nevertheless, there are still some challenges to be resolved, including the relatively low electrical conductivity and structural instability.

The incorporation of carbon-based materials presents a promising avenue to address these limitations, owing to their high surface area, exceptional electrical conductivity, and robust chemical stability. The preparation of hybrids using carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), can be an efficient strategy due to their high surface area, excellent electrical conductivity, and chemical stability. Specifically, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have gained significant interest due to their unique properties and potential applications in energy storage, sensors, bio-imaging, and other fields. Additionally, the heteroatoms doped GQDs using nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P) atoms create additional electronic states and modify the band structure, enhancing charge separation and transfer processes. Thus, phosphorus-doped GQDs (P-GQDs) exhibit better sensitivity and selectivity, making them more effective for various applications compared to GQDs doped with other heteroatoms. In our previous work, P-GQDs showed remarkable electrocatalytic activity in water electrolysis for H2 generation by modulating the bandgap and active sites through the incorporation of P-atoms in GQDs lattice [28]. The P-GQDs demonstrated enhanced electrocatalytic activity by improving the availability of active sites, charge transfer, and stability in seawater environments.

In this work, we demonstrated the synthesis of bismuth iron vanadate (BiFeV) and P-GQDs, and the incorporation of P-GQDs onto BiFeV as a hybrid nanocomposite to obtain active sites for efficient electrocatalysts of water splitting. First, we synthesized BiFeV with different Fe compositions to effectively tune and reduce impurity levels, achieving phase purity of BiFeV under specific Fe compositions. Then, the synthesized P-GQDs were incorporated onto BiFeV to prepare the P-GQDs/BiFeV composite (PG-BiFeV) through ultrasonication followed by probe sonication and hydrothermal analysis to form a hybrid electrocatalyst for water splitting (see Scheme 1 ). The prepared PG-BiFeV composite exhibited superior electrocatalytic activity for water splitting. These enhanced electrochemical performances of the PG-BiFeV composite were attributed to a higher number of electrochemically active areas due to the incorporation of P-GQDs, which also helped stabilize the active sites, preventing the degradation of the catalyst over time and enhancing overall performance.

Typically, 500 mg of dextrose and different amounts of phytic acid were added into 70 mL of DDW with sonication, then the mixture solution was transferred into 200 mL of a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. Then, the resulting solution mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane and further was purified by keeping it in a dialysis tube (Biotech CE-Tubing) for 24 h to remove the impurities and unreacted chemicals. The final product of P-GQDs was dispersed in ethanol by ultrasonication for 30 min. For a control sample, the pristine GQDs were prepared under the same procedure without the addition of phytic acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of BiFeV and PG-BiFeV composite
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of BiFeV, 2.91 g of Bi (NO3)3·5H2O and 2.80 g of NH4VO3 with different amounts of Fe (NO3)3·9H2O (1, 3, 5, and 7 wt%) were dissolved in 30 mL of DDW with 30 min sonication. Then, the solution mixture was transferred into 200 mL of a Teflon-lined autoclave for hydrothermal synthesis at 180 °C for 12 h. Later, the precipitate was collected and washed with ethanol and deionized water three times each and dried at 60 °C in a hot air oven. The final product of BiFeV was obtained from further calcination at 400 °C for 4 h. The BiFeV products obtained with varying Fe contents were labeled as BiFeV-x, where x represents the Fe content in BiFeV. For a control sample, BiV and FeV were synthesized through the same procedure without Fe (NO3)3·9H2O and Bi (NO3)3·5H2O, respectively. For the preparation of the PG-BiFeV composite, the synthesized BiFeVO4 and P-GQDs were stoichiometrically mixed in a 1:1 ratio in 1 mL of DI water with ultrasonication for 3 h at room temperature. The mixture suspension was transferred into 100 mL of Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The final product of the PG-BiFeV composite was collected by centrifugation several times with DI water and ethanol to remove large particles before drying at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was conducted using ULTIMA 4 X-ray diffractometer employing monochromatic Cu radiation (wavelength = 0.154 nm). The scanning rate was set at 2θ min−1, covering a range of 10–90°, with a duration of 30 min per sample. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed utilizing a K alpha model (X-Ray, 400 μm, Thermo Fisher). The morphologies of the catalyst surfaces were observed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) for elemental analysis. Raman spectra were acquired using the DXR3 Raman Microscope (Thermo Fisher Scientific) for finely powdered samples at room temperature with a 532 nm excitation source. The surface area, pore size distribution, and pore volume of prepared products were investigated under liquid N2 using Quadrasorbapparatus (Quantachrome Instruments) by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET).

The electrochemical measurement of prepared products for water splitting was carried out using a standard three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (Biologic VSP) at room temperature. For the working electrode, 4 mg of prepared electrode materials were dispersed in a solution of isopropanol and water (v/v 7:3) containing Nafion as the binder and then ultrasonicated to get a homogeneous catalyst slurry. Subsequently, 100 μL of slurry was coated onto a Ni foam (1 × 1 cm2) to prepare the working electrode as shown in Fig. S1. The Hg/HgO and a graphite rod were used as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. For convenience, the potentials measured for Hg/HgO were converted into potentials vs. RHE using the following equation;
E
(
v s .
RHE
)
= E
(
v s .
Hg / HgO
)
+
E 0
(
Hg / HgO
)
+ 0.0592
x
pH

Electrochemical measurements including linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and chronoamperometry were performed using 1.0 M KOH as the electrolyte (N2 saturated). The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed at 120 mV for HER and 490 mV for OER at the frequency range of 100 kHz to 100 mHz. Also, the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) was measured to evaluate the electrocatalytic activity from the double-layer capacitance values (Cdl) using the cyclic voltammetry curves at different scanning rates. The ECSA was calculated using the following equation;
ECSA =
C dl
/
C s
where Cdl and Cs are the double-layer capacitance and specific capacitance (0.035 mF cm−2), respectively [29]. For overall water splitting, a two-electrode system was used with the PG-BiFeV composite used as both the anode and the cathode.

X-ray diffraction analysis helps in understanding the phase purity and crystalline parameters of the samples prepared. Initially, the bismuth vanadate (BiV) and iron vanadate (FeV) were prepared to understand the compositional phase identification for the formation of BiFeV. The XRD peaks of BiV in Fig. S2a indicated that the synthesized BiV was formed as a monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 00-014-0688) with a major peak at 28.79 ᵒ (121) and minor peaks at 18.95ᵒ (011), 30.70 ᵒ (040), and 53.43ᵒ (161), with a lateral Bi-V-O bond formation composed with four oxygen atoms sharing the corners with one vanadium atom at a bond angle of 1.27 Å with a +4 valence state. Here the V-V dimers are attached to the metal atoms forming a M = O composition leading to a +4 and + 5 oxidation state based on London theory where the phase formation confirms that BiV has the potential to distribute the atomization while chemical bonding with a para-magnetic atom in its excited stated. Where the prepared FeV (Fig. S2b) stabilizes to orthorhombic FeVO4 (JCPDS No. 01-082-1871) phase with a major peak at 20.37ᵒ (001) and a minor peak at 21.85ᵒ (101), 26.25ᵒ (110), 31.21ᵒ (400), and 41.54ᵒ (002) indicating the formation of Fe-V-O bonds which are designated to FeVO4. Fig. 1 a shows the obtained XRD results for BiFeV, P-GQDs, and PG-BiFeV composite. The XRD patterns of BiFeV show the combination of monoclinic BiVO4 as the major phase and orthorhombic FeV as the minor phase. Through the valence sum method, the valence states of Bi, Fe, V, and O are calculated to be 1.78 +, 1.49+, 1.17+, and 0.91 respectively. Where the miller indices observed here match both BiV and FeV individual samples with a little distortion in phase shift which is due to the polarization effect of Fe reacting towards the V atom in its excited state leading to +3 and + 5 states. Observed miller indices for BiFeV are 18.67° (110), 18.98° (011), 28.58° (−130), 28.82° (−121), 28.94° (121), 30.54° (040), 35.22° (002), 46.74° (240), 47.31° (042), 53.24° (−161), and 53.31° (161) here the major peak appearing at 28° indicates the occupation of vacant atom by lateral perturbation effect where a phase-transition has occurred due to the tetravalent vacant vanadium atoms sharing with iron in +2 state leading to 3d 1 electronic configuration. This type of chemical bonding alters the crystal structure as the Wyckoff positions change with x, y, z Bi (1,0,0), Fe (0,0,0.25), V (0, 0, 0.25), and O (0, 0, ¼) with a fixed unit cell in its refined state. A broad spectrum indicating the characteristic peak of P-GQD with a major graphitic peak at 23.24ᵒ (002) is displayed in Fig. 1a corresponding to a d-spacing of ∼3.8 Å which is slightly larger than the typical interlayer spacing of graphite (±ê 3.35 Å). Here the increase in d-spacing can be attributed to the presence of P-atoms occupying the +4 state of C=O and C-M atoms leading to a distortion and peak shift in atomization, which might also be due to the amorphous graphitic nature of QDs. For the PG-BiFeV composite, a peak shift of the (002) plane is observed, moving from 23.24° to 20.32°, accompanied by an increase of peak intensity in the composite. Also, a slight intensity decrease is observed for other peaks in the composite. The shift signifies an increase in the interlayer spacing of the (002) planes in the P-GQDs and the incorporation of P-GQDs into the BiFeV matrix leads to the formation of strong bonds such as P-O-C covalent bonds and C-O-M bonds. These bonds enhance the structural stability and enhance the bi-lateral spacing between the graphene layers. In addition, the average crystalline size for the prepared products was calculated by using the Scherrer equation below [30].
D =
K λ
β
cos
θ
, where D is the crystallite size (nm), K is the Scherrer constant, λ is X-ray wavelength (Cu/Kα = 1.54 Å), β is full width at half maximum (FWHM) of XRD peak, and θ is Bragg angle. Also, the dislocation density (δ) and lattice strain (ɛ) for the prepared products were measured using the following equations, respectively [31].
δ =
1
D 2
;
ε =
β
4
tan
θ
; The obtained average crystalline size was found to be 21.73 and 26.75 nm for BiFeV and PG-BiFeV composite, respectively, where the larger crystalline size of PG-BiFeV than BiFeV indicates that the addition of P-GQDs reduces the lattice strain and dislocation density by acting as a nucleation site for crystalline growth. These results indicate a strong interaction between the P-GQDs and BiFeV matrix in forming a versatile composite. The obtained results of lattice strain, dislocation density, and full width at half maximum (FWHM) for the prepared products are summarized in Tables S1–S4. Raman spectroscopy measurement was performed to study the chemical structure and composition of the prepared products [32–34]. The obtained Raman spectra of BiV, FeV, and different BiFeV are presented in Fig. 1b. The Raman spectra of BiV exhibit a strong peak at 827 cm−1 with minor peaks at 374, 326, and 212 cm−1. The intense peak at 827 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the V-O bonds (V s V-O), while the minor peaks at 374 and 326, and 212 cm−1 are assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of (VO)4 3−, the lattice vibrations within the structure, respectively. Similarly, the Raman spectra of FeV show a strong peak at 821 cm−1 from the stretching vibrations of the V-O bonds with weak peaks at 724 and 632 cm−1 from the bridging mode of V-O-Fe and peaks at 476 and 309 cm−1 from the vibrational modes associated with symmetric and asymmetric (VO)4 3-. The Raman spectra for the mixed vanadate BiFeV display the intense peak around 806 cm−1 and the weak peaks around 330 and 190 cm−1, indicating a combination of vibrational modes from both BiV and FeV. With the increase of Fe content in the mixed vanadate BiFeV, the major peak around 820 cm−1 of the symmetric V-O stretching shifted to a lower frequency. The incorporation of Fe into the vanadate structure caused changes in the local environment of the V-O bonds due to a larger atomic size of Fe compared to V, which affects both bond lengths and angles. These modifications result in the reduction in the force constant of the V-O bonds, which leads to a decrease in the vibrational frequency. In addition, the Raman depth profiling spectra were measured for insights into the composition and structure of BiFeV beyond the surface. The Raman depth profiling of BiFeV-3 exhibits a nearly identical shape as shown in Fig. S3, suggesting a structural uniformity and chemical composition throughout the BiFeV material. It is also observed in the Raman spectra of P-GQDs (Fig. S4) that both pristine GQDs and P-GQDs show two distinct peaks at 1375 and 1590 cm−1 as D- and G-bands, associated with disordered structures and the sp 2 domain in the graphene, respectively. However, the slight peak shift toward lower wavelength is observed from 1375 to 1370 cm−1 for the D-band and 1590 to 1586 cm−1 for the G-band, which is attributed to a reduction in the number of defects and disrupts the symmetry of carbon lattice by the introduction of P-atoms into graphene lattice. The Raman spectra of the PG-BiFeV composite in Fig. 1b exhibit the combination of characteristic peaks of P-GQDs and BiFeV. To study further the peak position, width, and overlapped peaks, the Raman peaks are deconvoluted using the Lorentzian function. The decoration of P-GQDs on BiFeV influences the symmetric stretching of V-O bonds, leading the peak shift to a higher wavelength from 806 to 814 cm−1, because the introduction of P-GQDs induces the alterations of electronic structure and affects the vibrational properties like changes in bond length, or bond strengths. The Raman peaks of the PG-BiFeV composite at 203 and 345 cm−1 correspond to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching modes of (VO)4 3− tetrahedron. These results suggest the successful formation of P-GQDs anchored BiFeV composite.

The chemical composition of obtained products was evaluated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. The obtained XPS survey spectra of BiFeV exhibit the combination of BiV and FeV in Fig. S5. The XPS peaks for the synthesized products were deconvoluted using the Gaussian fittings. The pristine BiV shows major Bi4f peaks around 157.45 (Bi0), 158.63 (+3 7/2), 162.78 (Bi0), and 163.81 eV (+3 5/2). After Fe doping, a decrease in binding energy is observed where peaks are shifted to 156.15 (Bi0), 157.41 (+3 7/2), and 162.66 eV (+3 5/2). The increased electron density around Fe compared to V due to its higher electronegativity causes the effective nuclear charge experienced by bismuth core electrons to decrease slightly, leading to a reduction in binding energy. A similar trend was observed for all elements in both BiV and FeV when compared with BiFeV (Table S5). The XPS spectra of BiFeV, P-GQDs, and PG-BiFeV composite are displayed and compared in Fig. 2 . The XPS survey scan for BiFeV in Fig. 2a shows the distinct Bi4f, Fe2p (4.8 at%), V2p, and O1s atomic peaks. For P-GQDs, C1s and O1s peaks were observed with a weak P2p atomic peak (3.32 at%). The PG-BiFeV composite exhibits the combination of characteristic peaks from BiFeV and P-GQDs. The high-resolution Bi4f spectra of the PG-BiFeV composite deconvoluted into the two peaks of Bi3+ 4f7/2 and Bi3+ 4f5/2 peaks centred at 157.91 and 163.22 eV with the spin splitting of 5.31 eV, indicating the presence of Bi3+ ion state (Fig. 2b). When the P-GQDs were introduced with BiFeV, the combined effect of relatively high electronegativity of P-atom and delocalized π-electrons of P-GQDs withdraws the electrons from BiFeV, resulting in the reduction of local electron density and altering the shift in binding energies. The high resolution of Fe2p spectra for BiFeV and PG-BiFeV composite exhibit two major peaks, 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 with two satellite peaks (Fig. 2c). In the deconvoluted high-resolution spectra of V2p and O1s (Fig. 2d), the BiFeV and PG-BiFeV composite show the multiple peaks of V+3, V+4, and V+5 ion states and metal-oxygen (M − O) peak. In addition, the O-H peak at 532.02 eV is only observed for the PG-BiFeV composite due to the presence of P-GQDs. The binding energy peaks in the P2p of P-GQDs were found at 132.41 and 138.64 eV which correspond to the P-C bonds and P-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2f). The distinct peak at 141 eV indicates the higher oxidation state of phosphorous bonded to multiple oxygen atoms. It is also observed in the PG-BiFeV composite with two distinct peaks at 132.04 and 133.53 eV for P-C and P-O, respectively. Also, the BiFeV lattice might stabilize the P-atom state and prevent the highly oxidized state, resulting in the disappearance of peaks at 141 eV. In addition, the C1s peak can be split into 283.4 (C=C/C-C), 284.81 (C-O/C-P), 287.28 (C-O-C), and 289.72 eV (C=O) (Fig. 2f). These XPS results suggest the retention of graphitic structure and the stability of composite by the bond formation, which promotes the efficient charge transfer in the PG-BiFeV composite.

The morphological study for the prepared BiFeV and PG-BiFeV composite was performed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) combined with the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis. The FE-SEM image of BiFeV in Fig. 3 a shows that a mixture of sub-micrometer-sized rods and particles was synthesized. The elemental mapping images of BiFeV by EDS analysis exhibit the uniform distribution for each element of B, Fe, V, and O atoms, confirming the successful synthesis of BiFeV (Fig. S6). The FE-SEM images of the PG-BiFeV composite in Fig. 3b show a similar morphological microstructure, and it is hard to observe the presence of P-GQDs in the composite due to their nanometre size. In addition, the EDS elemental mapping images of the PG-BiFeV composite exhibit the uniform distribution of components including Bi, Fe, V, O, P, and C atoms, confirming the uniform deposition of P-GQDs in the BiFeV (Fig. 3c). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) proved the formation of PG-BiFeV by demonstrating the anchoring of BiFeV on P-GQDs as this incorporation of QDs has reduced the particle aggregation. TEM images of BiFeV (Fig. S7) show the road shaped with an interplanar spacing from 0.29 to 0.438 nm, corresponding to the (101), (121), and (040) facets with clear lattice fringe, indicating the rod formation strengthening XRD results. TEM images in Fig. 3d–e shows the anchoring of quasi-spherical PGQDs onto the peripheral rods of the BiFeV structure, stating the formation of PG-BiFeV composite and the SAED pattern of PG-BiFeV in Fig. 3f findings matches the BiFeV and P-GQDs facets. The retention of a mixed structure with sub-micrometer-sized rods and particles in the composite suggests that the P-GQDs are well dispersed with BiFeV without aggregation. The surface area of catalysts plays a crucial role in electrochemistry by enhancing reaction rates, improving mass transfer, facilitating charge transfer, and maximizing the availability of active sites for reactant interactions. To compare, the surface area of as-prepared samples was analyzed using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method (Fig. S8 and Table S6). The surface area of BiV was measured to be 4.87 m2 g−1, while that of BiFeV was 9 m2 g−1 after the incorporation of PGQDs, the surface area increased significantly to 17.10 m2 g−1, with a reduction in pore radius and an increase in pore volume. This suggests that the incorporation of QGs enhances the material's surface properties due to better dispersion of QDs and the formation of additional sites.

The electrochemical activity of prepared products for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) was investigated using a three-electrode configuration in 1.0 M KOH. The electrochemical activity of BiFeV products with the different Fe ratios was first evaluated. The obtained linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of HER and OER at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 for different BiFeV products in Figs. S9a–b show lower overpotential for BiFeV-3 than other BiFeV products at a current density of 100 mA cm−2. Also, the electrochemical active surface area (ECSA) investigation for the different BiFeV products presents a higher ECSA value of BiFeV-3 than others (Fig. S9c). Thus, the PG-BiFeV composite was prepared using the BiFeV-3 product, and its electrocatalytic activity for HER was evaluated. The commercial catalyst of Pt/C was also tested and compared under the same experimental condition. The LSV curves are obtained within a range of 0 V–0.6 V with respect to RHE at a rate of 5 mV s−1 in N2 saturated electrolyte (1.0 M KOH). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the PG-BiFeV composite exhibits a lower overpotential of 341 mV at a current density of 100 mA∙cm−2 than BiV (417 mV) and BiFeV (380 mV), which is comparable to the commercial Pt/C catalyst (348 mV). It can be also observed in the comparison between BiV and BiFeV that the Fe doping in BiV enhances the electrochemical activity. To understand the reaction kinetics and the rate-determining step of BiFeV for HER, the Tafel analysis was conducted. The obtained Tafel plots for prepared products are shown in Fig. 4b. A lower Tafel slope value and better exchange current density were observed for the PG-BiFeV composite compared to BiV, BiFeV, and Pt/C (Table S6). The calculated values of the PG-BiFeV composite were determined to be 185 mV·dec−1 and 1.60 mA cm−2, indicating that the HER reaction kinetics of the PG-BiFeV composite follows the Volmer-Heyrovsky pathway with the Volmer step as the rate-determining step [35]. The interface reactions between electrolyte and electrode were analyzed using the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) analysis, which supports the lower overpotential and Tafel slop value of the composite. As shown in Fig. 4c, the smaller diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot implies a lower charge transfer resistance (Rct), indicating a faster reaction rate with low resistance during the HER process. The obtained BiV shows a relatively high Rct of 1.93 Ω, and the BiFeV exhibits an improved Rct of 1.69 Ω. This reduction in charge transfer resistance is due to the incorporation of Fe, which promotes better charge transfer kinetics. For the PG-BiFeV composite, the lowest Rct value of 1.36 Ω is obtained, which suggests that the incorporation of P-GQDs in BiFeV provides additional pathways for electron transfer and more active sites for the catalytic reaction. The CV curves of BiV, BiFeV, and PG-BiFeV composite were collected in non-faradaic regions at different scan rates from 20 to 100 mV s−1 with a potential window of 0.04–0.16 V vs RHE (Fig. 4d and S10). As shown in Fig. 4e, the PG-BiFeV composite exhibits an enhanced Cdl value of 1.74 mF and the calculated ECSA value of 49.71 cm2, compared to those of BiV (0.78 mF and 22.3 cm2) and BiFeV (1.2 mF and 34.28 cm2). These results indicate that the enhanced HER activity of the PG-BiFeV composite is attributed to the synergistic effect of P-GQDs and BiFeV, including the abundant electrochemically active sites and efficient reaction kinetics. The HER performance of the PG-BiFeV composite was compared with previously reported other reports using vanadate-based catalysts, indicating the superior HER performance of the PG-BiFeV composite (Table S7).

In addition, the cycle stability and durability of the PG-BiFeV composite were investigated through the chronoamperometry test for 100 h at a constant potential of 0.20 V. As shown in Fig. 5 a, a negligible change in current density is observed for the PG-BiFeV composite, while the commercial Pt/C catalyst displays a noticeable current change. Furthermore, the durability of the composite was determined using cyclic voltammetry methods at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1, and the polarization curves before and after 2000 cycles with minimal change are shown in Fig. 5b. Additionally, the chronoamperometry measurements at the different applied potentials were conducted to evaluate the practical stability of PG-BiFeV composite [36], and the obtained results in Fig. S11a present the excellent stability of PG-BiFeV composite at higher current densities for practical HER application. The XPS spectra of the PG-BiFeV composite after HER measurement are shown in Fig. S12 and Table S8. The Bi4f peaks shifted to higher binding energies with larger FWHM after HER measurements along with an additional tiny peak corresponding to the Bi-H/Bi-H2O. The Fe2p peaks a slight shift towards higher binding energy indicating interaction with electrolyte, showing participation in electron transport. However, within V2p envelope, the tiny +3 peak was observed to disappear after scanning whereas the corresponding O1s showed an increment in the M − O peak indicating higher M − O interaction. P1s peak was fitted to doublets at an unaltered position. The results indicate Bi to the active site. Although the disappearance of the V 3+ peak indicated its participation, the post-OER studies also indicated the disappearance of that peak indicating the phenomena is the result of scanning at higher pH. Further, the XPS analysis after the chronoamperometry measurements for different potentials of 0.27 and 0.34 V shows similar oxidation state change of elements (Figs. S13 and S14). In addition, the XRD pattern of the PG-BiFeV composite after HER measurements retained a similar spectrum (Fig. S15). The formation of intermediates and adsorption/desorption primarily occur at the surface of the catalysts, which do not affect the bulk crystal structure, supporting the long-term stability of the PG-BiFeV composite catalyst. These results suggest the superior cycle stability and durability of PG-BiFeV composite for HER application.

To investigate the bifunctional electrocatalytic activity of the PG-BiFeV composite, the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) measurements were also carried out for the prepared products in 1.0 M KOH with a three-electrode configuration. In control, the commercial catalyst of IrO2 for OER was examined and compared under the same experimental condition. As shown in Fig. 6 a, it can be seen in the LSV curves of OER that the PG-BiFeV composite exhibits a lower overpotential of 396 mV at a current density of 100 mA∙cm−2 than BiV (524 mV), BiFeV-3 (447 mV), and a commercial IrO2 catalyst (526 mV). In addition, the anodic peaks are found in the LSV curves prior to OER response, and higher peak current with potential shift is observed for PG-BiFeV composite. These peaks are ascribed to the formation of metal hydroxide or metal oxide which is essential for the enhanced catalytic activities of OER. A higher peak current at a higher potential indicates more active sites and a stabilized state of the PG-BiFeV composite. The corresponding Tafel plots of the PG-BiFeV composite in Fig. 6b also demonstrate the lower Tafel slope value of 62 mV·dec−1 and higher exchange current density of 0.09 mA cm−2, compared to other products (Table S6). The obtained Rct values for BiV, BiFeV, and PG-BiFeV composite from EIS measurement are 0.71, 0.62, and 0.57 Ω, respectively (Fig. 6c). The reduced Rct values of PG-BiFeV composite indicate the improvement in charge transfer resistance and efficiency of PG-BiFeV composite for the OER process. The CV curves of BiV, BiFeV, and PG-BiFeV composite were also collected in non-faradaic regions at different scan rates from 20 to 100 mV s−1 with a potential window of 0.92–1.07 V (Fig. 6d and Fig. S16). As shown in Fig. 6e, the PG-BiFeV composite exhibits a higher Cdl value of 1.79 mF and the calculated ECSA value of 51.14 cm2, compared to those of BiV (0.78 mF and 22.35 cm2) and BiFeV (1.03 mF and 29.42 cm2). This superior OER performance of the PG-BiFeV composite is attributed to the abundant electrochemically active sites and efficient reaction kinetics by incorporating P-GQDs. The excellent electrocatalytic activities of P-GQDs, compared to the pristine GQDs, were further explored by the polarization curves and Nyquist plots toward HER and OER (Fig. S17). The density functional theory (DFT) study in our previous work also reported that the various P-C bonds in P-GQDs facilitate the adsorption and desorption of reaction intermediates for HER and OER rather than the pristine GQDs, resulting the improved efficiency of water electrolysis. This superiority in the performance of the PG-BiFeV composite is further confirmed in the comparison with other reported vanadate-based catalysts, as shown in Table S9.

The stability and durability of the PG-BiFeV composite for OER were also evaluated through chronoamperometry at a constant potential of 0.15 V for 100 h of continuous operation. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the minor decay is only observed for the PG-BiFeV composite, while the significant degradation of current density is recorded for the IrO2 catalyst, suggesting the superior cycle stability of the PG-BiFeV composite. The stability of composites was further assessed via CV with the same conditions and Fig. 7b shows stable polarization curves even after 2000 cycles. Furthermore, the chronoamperometry curves at the different applied voltages demonstrate the superior OER stability of PG-BiFeV composite even high current densities, which provides the potential for practical application (Fig. S11b). The chemical change of the PG-BiFeV composite after long-term OER measurements was studied by XPS analysis (Fig. S12 and Table S8). As the OER progresses, various reaction intermediates, including OH∗, O∗, and OOH∗, form through the breaking and formation of chemical bonds. Furthermore, the adsorption of these intermediates onto the catalyst surface can induce surface modifications, which results in shifts in XPS peaks. As shown in Fig. S12a, Bi4f peaks showed an almost unaltered position with comparatively higher FWHM. The Fe2p peaks showed a shift towards higher binding energy along with an increment in O-M peak intensity indicating Fe-O participating actively for OER by interaction with OH, OOH, and O intermediates. The corresponding 2p peak showed a shift at a higher binding energy region with increasing V-O and the disappearance of the initial V3+ peak within the 3/2 envelope, which can be attributed to the alkaline electrolyte condition. The C1s and P1s peaks did not show any significant alteration but the fact the presence of PGQD increased the activity indicated its contribution to electron transport and stability. The further XPS study after the chronoamperometry test at different potentials of 0.34 and 0.39V shows similar peak change of variation of individual oxidation states and confirmed Fe-O as the active site aided by the neighbouring electronegative atoms (Figs. S13 and S14). Also, the XRD analysis after the OER test in Fig. S15 shows that the XRD patterns of the PG-BiFeV composite after OER measurements remain mostly unchanged from the fresh state before the OER test, suggesting the structural durability of the PG-BiFeV composite. These results indicate that the prepared PG-BiFeV composite demonstrates superior bifunctional electrocatalytic activities for both HER and OER with excellent stability.

Based on the superior bifunctional HER and OER electrocatalytic activities of PG-BiFeV composite, the symmetric two-electrode water electrolysis cell was fabricated using PG-BiFeV composites as both cathode and anode as shown in Fig. 8 a. For comparison, the commercial Pt/C and IrO2 catalysts as the cathode and anode were also tested under the same experimental condition. In the overall water splitting using PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes, the vigorous formation of O2 and H2 bubbles was observed on the surface of both PG-BiFeV composite electrodes (Fig. 8b, Fig. S18, and Video S1-S2), where the bubbles are formed uniformly on most area of Ni foam electrode. Also, the generated bubbles are rapidly detached and rise from the Ni foam electrode to the water surface without any dangling, which is important to electrolysis efficiency because the bubbles significantly affect the transport of dissolved hydrogen molecules and ions near the electrode. These results suggest the successful operation of the water electrolyzer using the PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes. The LSV curves of overall water splitting in Fig. 8c show the lower overall cell voltage of 2.04 V for the PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes at a current density of 50 mA cm−2 than the commercial Pt/C‖IrO2 electrodes (2.27 V). In addition, the chronoamperometry measurement was performed at a constant potential of 0.41 V to investigate the durability of the PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes electrolyzer. The chronoamperometry curves in Fig. 8d exhibit that the PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes demonstrate only a slight decay of current density over 100 h of electrolysis operation, and the inset shows the marginal change of HER LSV curve after the chronoamperometry measurement. To evaluate the feasibility of PG-BiFeV composite for the practical application, we further tested the overall splitting of PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes using the H-cell reactor where a membrane separated the electrodes. The resulting LSV curve and chronoamperometry curve for 50 h operation exhibit excellent stability (Fig. S19), suggesting that the prepared PG-BiFeV catalyst provides potential as bifunctional electrodes for a practical application in overall water splitting. These results indicate that the PG-BiFeV composite offers superior stability and efficiency for overall water electrolysis.
Supplementary data related to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpowsour.2025.236999

The following are the Supplementary data related to this article.
Video 1 Video 1
Video 2 Video 2

In summary, the BiFeV with different iron ratios was successfully synthesized and their composite with P-GQDs was then prepared for the electrocatalysts of water splitting. The prepared PG-BiFeV composite demonstrates excellent electrocatalytic activities for both HER and OER with superior stability. The PG-BiFeV composite performed small overpotentials of 341 mV for HER and 396 mV for OER at the current density of 100 mA cm−2 with excellent stability for 100 h operation with negligible decay, compared with commercial catalysts. Using the bifunctional catalytic activities of PG-BiFeV composite, the overall water splitting was investigated using the PG-BiFeV composites as both cathode and anode. The electrolyzer using PG-BiFeV‖PG-BiFeV electrodes demonstrated superior water-splitting performance with lower cell voltage and good stability. The prepared PG-BiFeV composite in this work not only provides perspectives on developing efficient electrocatalysts but also offers the potential for the practical performance of overall water splitting.

Beena Mol Babu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Min Jae Kim: Methodology, Data curation. Tata Sanjay Kanna Sharma: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Won Mook Choi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the 2025 Research Fund of the University of Ulsan.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Gotic, S. Music, M. Ivanda, M. Soufek, S. Popovic, Synthesis and characterisation of bismuth (III) vanadate, J. Mol. Struct., 744-747 (2005) 535-540.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insight on tafel slopes from a microkinetic analysis of aqueous electrocatalysis for energy conversion
Sci. Rep.

Prog. Mater. Sci.
